adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep Hono and replace the scattered hand-rolled middleware wiring with a small typed middleware module in packages/api that exports createMiddleware-based building blocks plus a single applyHttpMiddleware(app) composition point. Standardize the order as request ID and tracing context, structured pino request logging, problem+json error handling, cookie/session or API-key auth, token-bucket rate limiting, and finally route-level Zod validation. Add typed Hono context variables for session, principal, API key, rate-limit metadata, request ID and logger so handlers stop re-parsing request state. Split routes into explicit public, session-authenticated and API-key-authenticated groups so middleware selection is declarative instead of copied per route. Cover ordering and failure-shape regressions with node:test integration tests against the Hono app, including unauthenticated, rate-limited and thrown-error cases.
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing Hono with Express, Fastify, NestJS or another HTTP framework is not proposed because the project has already standardized on Hono for auth, logging and rate-limiting middleware across Node and edge runtimes.

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Redux Toolkit and instead clean up apps/dashboard state within the existing Zustand convention. We can split the current store into small domain stores or slices for session, filters, toasts, and any new feature state, add typed selectors/actions, and keep server-derived data out of global state where possible. For more complex flows, we should introduce explicit action helpers and tests around store transitions using node:test rather than adding a second global state framework. This keeps the dashboard aligned with the repo’s lightweight state model while addressing the maintainability issue directly.

reply.proposal: I do not recommend setting up Prisma in packages/api because it conflicts with the existing decision to query Postgres via postgres.js and hand-written SQL to keep query plans visible and avoid a runtime abstraction layer. Instead, I would tighten our current pattern by adding a small db module with typed query helpers, colocated row types/mappers, and repository functions per domain so callers get type-safe results without embedding SQL throughout handlers. For any unsafe inputs, we should continue validating at the API boundary with Zod and bind all query parameters through postgres.js template tags. I would also add node:test coverage for each repository query against the docker-compose Postgres service, including mapper behavior and representative edge cases.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM / Prisma Client, because the project has already chosen postgres.js with hand-written SQL to avoid a runtime abstraction layer and keep SQL/query plans directly reviewable

reply.proposal: Implement a small billing-provider client module inside packages/api that wraps native fetch with our existing retry/timeout helper, rather than introducing a new HTTP client dependency. The module should expose typed methods per billing endpoint, validate outbound request config and inbound response bodies with Zod, and translate provider failures into our standard problem+json errors with provider request IDs, status codes, and safe diagnostic details included in pino logs. Retries should be limited to idempotent calls and transient failures such as 408, 429, and 5xx, using exponential backoff with jitter and a hard per-request timeout via AbortController. Configuration such as base URL, API key, timeout, and retry count should come from environment variables, with secrets never logged. Add node:test unit tests using mocked fetch responses to cover success, timeout, retry exhaustion, non-retryable errors, and malformed provider responses.
acknowledged_rejections: Do not introduce Axios, Got, Ky, or another HTTP client library because the project standard is native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP.

reply.proposal: We should keep session tokens exclusively in Secure httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies set by the API via Set-Cookie, not expose them to JavaScript. The SPA should call the API with native fetch and rely on the browser to attach cookies automatically; for same-origin calls the default credentials behavior is sufficient, and for a separate mobile web origin/subdomain we should use credentials: 'include' with explicit CORS allowlist and Allow-Credentials on the Hono API. This preserves our existing XSS protection boundary while still letting the SPA make authenticated requests without manually attaching Authorization headers. If the mobile web team needs a JS-readable value for request correlation or CSRF-style validation, we can issue a separate non-secret token, but it must not be the session token.
acknowledged_rejections: Making the session token readable from JavaScript, including storing it in localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, React/Zustand state, or an in-memory SPA variable, because the project already decided session tokens must stay in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies to keep them out of reach of injected scripts.

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Jest to packages/api because the project has already standardized on the built-in node:test runner to avoid test-framework dependencies and CI transform configuration. Instead, we should add request-handler unit tests using node:test, assert/strict, and Hono's app.request() or handler-level invocation so tests exercise the same routing and middleware path without spinning up a server. For TypeScript, we can keep the existing build-first flow and run node --test against compiled output, adding package scripts in packages/api for test and test:watch if needed. Where handlers depend on Postgres or outbound HTTP, we should inject those dependencies behind small interfaces and use lightweight fakes in unit tests, reserving docker compose Postgres coverage for integration tests.

reply.proposal: Keep the marketing pages on the existing Tailwind-first styling approach, but organize styles by component so the designer gets colocated ownership without adding a parallel styling system. Each marketing component should live in its own folder or file with Tailwind utility classes inline or in a local `styles`/slot class map for readability, with shared marketing primitives like `Hero`, `Section`, `CTAButton`, and `LogoCloud` kept under the marketing route. Extend the existing Tailwind config only for reusable design tokens such as colors, spacing, typography scale, and animations, rather than creating page-specific global CSS. Use global CSS only for true app-wide concerns, and avoid runtime CSS-in-JS or a second component styling abstraction.
acknowledged_rejections: Component-scoped CSS Modules or CSS-in-JS as the primary approach, because the project has already standardized dashboard styling on Tailwind utility classes with no runtime style computation

- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Implement the shared session cache as a Postgres UNLOGGED table accessed from the Hono auth middleware via postgres.js, keyed by a SHA-256 hash of the session token rather than the raw token. The middleware should read the cache first and return the compact session payload needed by downstream handlers; on miss, it should load from the authoritative session/user tables, validate expiry and revocation state, then upsert the cache row with a bounded TTL. Logout, token rotation, permission changes, and account disablement should explicitly delete affected cache rows, while expired entries can be removed by a small periodic cleanup query. Because the cache is in Postgres, every API instance sees the same session data without adding another datastore, and because it is UNLOGGED, losing it on database restart is acceptable and self-healing through misses. Add OpenTelemetry spans and pino fields for cache hit/miss/stale/error so we can verify the latency improvement and tune TTLs safely.
acknowledged_rejections: Redis or another external cache service for sessions, because the project already decided to keep the stack to one datastore and use Postgres UNLOGGED tables for session caching.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: I recommend a guarded migration-generation workflow rather than runtime auto-migrations: introduce a canonical declarative schema under db/schema.sql or db/schema/*.sql, use Atlas to diff it against the current migration state, and emit the next numbered forward-only SQL migration file. The generated SQL remains checked in and reviewed as literal SQL, and production continues to run the existing migration runner against committed files only. CI will spin up Postgres via docker compose, apply migrations, and verify that the declarative schema and migration history are in sync, with destructive diffs requiring an explicit PR annotation. We can bootstrap the schema definition from the current database using pg_dump --schema-only, then add pnpm scripts and README instructions for the new workflow.
acknowledged_rejections: Runtime auto-migration or schema-sync that applies generated diffs directly in production; prior decisions require reviewed, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations.
Replacing postgres.js and hand-written application queries with an ORM solely to obtain migration generation; prior decisions keep database access as direct SQL with typed row mappers.

reply.proposal: Add sparse fieldsets to the existing REST API using a query parameter like `fields[users]=id,name&fields[projects]=id,status`, documented per endpoint in the OpenAPI spec. Each endpoint should whitelist selectable fields in its Zod request schema, map requested public field names to explicit SQL columns, and return only those properties in the response while preserving the existing resource shape. For list endpoints, we should also ensure required identity/linking fields are always included when needed for pagination, caching, or relationship assembly. This keeps the public API RESTful, avoids a new query runtime, and lets mobile screens request exactly the fields they render.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL is not proposed because the project has already decided the public API stays REST and sparse fieldsets address the overfetching complaint.
